By leveraging shared parameters and pretraining techniques, the model demonstrates significant improvements over existing state-of-the-art methods in relation extraction tasks, achieving notable reductions in error rates on benchmark datasets.

The article presents AllenNLP, a deep learning platform designed for natural language processing (NLP) that focuses on semantic representation and reasoning. It discusses the platform's capabilities in various NLP tasks, emphasizing its applications in semantic evaluation and processing within the field of computational linguistics.

The article presents CodeBERT, a pre-trained model designed to enhance the understanding and generation of both programming and natural languages, highlighting its applications in natural language processing and machine learning within the field of computer science.

The article explores the phenomenon of text degeneration in deep neural language models, highlighting how these models, despite high-quality training, often produce bland and repetitive outputs. It discusses the distributional differences between human and machine-generated text and introduces Nucleus Sampling as an effective method to enhance text diversity and coherence without compromising fluency.

The article presents a novel approach to sequence labeling using contextual string embeddings derived from recurrent neural networks, which model character distributions without explicit word definitions. The proposed embeddings demonstrate superior performance in various sequence labeling tasks, including named entity recognition, achieving new state-of-the-art F1-scores and providing a framework for further research and application.

Stanza is an open-source Python toolkit for natural language processing that supports 66 languages, offering a fully neural pipeline for various text analysis tasks such as tokenization, lemmatization, and named entity recognition. It demonstrates competitive performance across multiple languages and includes integration with Stanford CoreNLP for additional functionalities.

This article addresses the challenges of end-to-end scene text recognition in natural images, highlighting the limitations of traditional OCR methods and presenting two systems for evaluation. The study demonstrates that a generic object recognition approach outperforms state-of-the-art techniques, suggesting new possibilities for applying advanced methods in real-world text recognition scenarios.

The article explores the minimum description length principle and stochastic complexity in the context of data compression and statistical modeling, highlighting their applications in areas such as context tree modeling, density estimation, and Gaussian linear regression. It discusses the performance of various coding methods, including normalized maximized likelihood and predictive codings, in achieving optimal solutions for universal coding problems.

The article discusses the 2021 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing (EMNLP 2021), highlighting its hybrid format that combines virtual and in-person participation in Punta Cana, Dominican Republic. It emphasizes the challenges and opportunities of organizing such a conference, while acknowledging the contributions of the organizing team and the importance of remote participation for inclusivity.

The article discusses a novel approach to opinion mining that focuses on lexicon expansion and target extraction using a method called double propagation, which leverages syntactic relations identified through dependency parsing. The proposed semi-supervised technique demonstrates significant improvements over existing methods in extracting opinion targets from text, particularly in the context of product reviews.

The article presents mT5, a massively multilingual pre-trained text-to-text transformer model designed for natural language processing tasks. It explores the model's architecture, training methodology, and its effectiveness in handling multiple languages, contributing to advancements in multilingual text mining and deep learning.

The article presents a novel deep convolutional neural network designed for sentiment analysis of short texts, addressing the challenges posed by limited contextual information. By leveraging character-to-sentence level features, the proposed model achieves state-of-the-art accuracy on two distinct corpora, including movie reviews and Twitter messages.

The article explores the debate on whether words are read through visual or phonological processes by presenting a large-scale computational model that integrates both approaches. It highlights how this model, which employs a cooperative division of labor between visual and phonological components, effectively accounts for various behavioral phenomena in reading and language comprehension.

The article discusses a Ph.D. thesis from the Massachusetts Institute of Technology that explores the development of structural descriptions through examples, focusing on applications in computer science, natural language processing, and machine learning. It highlights the intersection of deep learning and description logic in creating effective models for understanding and generating structured information.

The article presents a novel sentence-level attention-based model for distant supervised relation extraction, addressing the challenges posed by noisy data and incorrect labeling. By utilizing convolutional neural networks and implementing selective attention over multiple instances, the model effectively enhances the extraction performance, as demonstrated by significant improvements in experimental results on real-world datasets.

The article explores an innovative end-to-end approach for large vocabulary continuous speech recognition (LVCSR) that utilizes an attention-based mechanism within a Recurrent Neural Network (RNN) to model input-output alignments, offering a viable alternative to traditional hybrid systems that combine neural networks with Hidden Markov Models (HMMs). The proposed method demonstrates comparable accuracy to existing HMM-free RNN models while enhancing operational efficiency through the integration of n-gram language models.

The article discusses a novel, unsupervised approach to generating sentence embeddings that significantly outperforms more complex methods, particularly in scenarios with limited training data. By utilizing weighted average vectors from an unlabeled corpus like Wikipedia and applying PCA/SVD for modification, this method achieves a 10% to 30% improvement in textual similarity tasks, establishing a strong baseline for future research in natural language processing.

The article presents a method for clustering English words based on their distribution in specific syntactic contexts, utilizing relative frequency distributions and entropy as a similarity measure. It explores how these clusters can represent coarse sense distinctions and employs deterministic annealing to create hierarchical clusters, evaluated through word co-occurrence models.

The article discusses a bootstrapping process that develops extraction patterns for subjective expressions by utilizing high-precision classifiers to label unannotated data, thereby creating a large training set for a pattern learning algorithm. This approach enhances the identification of opinionated sentences, improving recall while preserving high precision in natural language processing tasks.

This article presents a machine learning approach to coreference resolution of noun phrases in unrestricted text, utilizing a small annotated corpus to effectively resolve various types of noun phrases, including general phrases and specific entity types. The evaluation on standard datasets demonstrates that the proposed system achieves accuracy comparable to traditional non-learning methods, marking it as a promising advancement in the field of natural language processing.

The article introduces ViLBERT, a model that extends the BERT architecture to create task-agnostic joint representations of visual and textual data through a multi-modal two-stream approach. By pretraining on a large dataset and applying it to various vision-and-language tasks, ViLBERT achieves state-of-the-art performance, marking a significant advancement in the integration of vision and language processing.

The article presents a Tensor Fusion Network designed for multimodal sentiment analysis, which integrates language with other modalities like gestures and voice to capture both intra-modality and inter-modality dynamics. The proposed model demonstrates superior performance over existing state-of-the-art methods in analyzing sentiment from online videos.

The article presents a novel approach to collocational analysis that integrates grammatical structures with lexemes, emphasizing the interaction between words and their associated constructions. This construction-based method aims to enhance the description of language by objectively identifying meaning constructions and their preferred lexeme slots, while also contributing to theoretical advancements in applied linguistics.

The article discusses an enhanced version of the SentiStrength algorithm for detecting sentiment strength in social media texts, particularly on platforms like Twitter and MySpace. It highlights the algorithm's effectiveness in identifying direct sentiment indicators across various datasets, outperforming baseline methods in both supervised and unsupervised scenarios, and suggests its applicability in diverse contexts within sentiment analysis.

The article discusses a statistical approach to improving the mechanized encoding and searching of literary information, highlighting the challenges of conveying meaning through written communication and the variability in individual expression. It outlines a systematic method that includes document analysis, vocabulary establishment, and the use of automated encoding and search techniques to enhance information retrieval in literature.

The article explores the prediction of semantic orientation in adjectives by analyzing conjunctions that indicate positive or negative sentiment. Utilizing a log-linear regression model and clustering algorithms, the study achieves high accuracy in classifying adjectives based on their orientations, demonstrating effective performance in natural language processing tasks.

The article presents the Universal Sentence Encoder for English, a tool designed to enhance natural language processing tasks by providing high-quality sentence embeddings, facilitating applications such as machine translation and linguistic analysis. It details the methodology and effectiveness of the encoder in various NLP scenarios, as demonstrated at the 2018 Conference on Empirical Methods in Natural Language Processing.

The article discusses the growing importance of detecting abusive language in online user-generated content and critiques current methods that rely on blacklists and regular expressions. It presents a novel machine learning approach that outperforms existing deep learning techniques and introduces a unique annotated corpus for language, while also analyzing user behavior over time to improve detection accuracy.

The article introduces SUBTLEX-UK, a comprehensive word frequency database derived from British television subtitles, which offers improved insights into lexical decision times compared to existing resources. It also presents new measures of contextual diversity and frequency, aiming to enhance linguistic research and understanding of British English.

The article introduces a novel biterm topic model (BTM) designed to effectively uncover topics in short texts, such as tweets and instant messages, by addressing the limitations of conventional topic models that struggle with data sparsity. Through extensive experiments, the BTM demonstrates superior performance in discovering coherent topics compared to baseline methods, highlighting its potential for broader applications in content analysis.

This article provides an overview of neural network models in the context of natural language processing (NLP), highlighting their recent advancements and applications in text analysis. It serves as a tutorial for researchers, covering essential techniques such as input encoding, feed-forward networks, and automatic gradient computation.

The article explores how BERT, a language representation model, learns and captures the structural elements of the English language through a series of experiments, revealing a hierarchical organization of linguistic information across its layers and highlighting its ability to handle complex language features such as subject-verb agreement and long-term dependencies.

The article explores the concept of affect labeling, or putting feelings into words, and its impact on managing negative emotions through a functional magnetic resonance imaging study. It highlights how this process reduces activity in the amygdala and limbic regions while increasing activity in the right ventrolateral prefrontal cortex, suggesting a neurocognitive pathway that may help diminish emotional reactivity.

The article presents a stochastic parts program and a noun phrase parser designed for processing unrestricted text, contributing to advancements in natural language processing and computational linguistics. Authored by Kenneth Ward Church, it discusses methodologies for syntactic parsing and keyword extraction within the context of applied natural language processing.

The article presents a multi-context deep learning framework for saliency detection, addressing the limitations of traditional low-level cues in challenging visual scenarios. By utilizing Convolutional Neural Networks to integrate both global and local context, the proposed method demonstrates significant improvements over existing state-of-the-art techniques across multiple datasets.

The article discusses the CoNLL-X shared task on multilingual dependency parsing, detailing the conversion of treebanks for 13 languages into a dependency format and evaluating the parsing performance. It also explores various approaches used by participants, aiming to draw conclusions about the challenges and complexities of parsing different languages and annotation schemes.

The article presents a Neural Responding Machine designed for short-text conversations, exploring its applications in natural language processing and spoken language technology. It discusses the integration of neural networks and deep learning techniques to enhance communication and cognitive interaction in conversational systems.

The article presents a multimodal Recurrent Neural Network (m-RNN) model designed for generating image captions by modeling the probability distribution of words based on previous words and images. The effectiveness of the m-RNN, which integrates deep recurrent neural networks and convolutional networks, is demonstrated through superior performance on benchmark datasets and its application in image and sentence retrieval tasks.

The article discusses the integration of deep reinforcement learning into dialogue generation for conversational agents, addressing the limitations of traditional models that generate responses in isolation. By employing policy gradient methods, the study demonstrates how to create more coherent, informative, and engaging dialogues, ultimately enhancing the ability of chatbots to sustain meaningful conversations.

The article introduces VisualBERT, a streamlined and effective framework for tackling various vision-and-language tasks by utilizing stacked Transformer layers for implicit alignment of text and image elements. Through pre-training on caption data and evaluation across multiple tasks, VisualBERT demonstrates competitive performance against state-of-the-art models while maintaining simplicity and the ability to ground language without explicit supervision.

The article discusses the development of the **tm** package in R, which serves as a comprehensive framework for text mining applications, highlighting its capabilities in statistical and machine learning methods for tasks such as count-based analysis, clustering, and classification. It provides an overview of the tools available in R for text processing and knowledge discovery in the field of natural language processing.

The article discusses the use of a probabilistic Earley parser as a psycholinguistic model to analyze cognitive load in human sentence processing, focusing on how it predicts reading times based on the probability of structural options disconfirmed during sentence comprehension. It highlights the model's effectiveness in explaining processing phenomena such as garden path ambiguity and subject/object relative asymmetry, supported by corpus-frequency data.

The article presents "extreme summarization," a novel single-document summarization task that emphasizes abstractive methods to generate concise, one-sentence news summaries. It introduces a topic-aware convolutional neural network model trained on a large dataset from the BBC, demonstrating its effectiveness in capturing long-range dependencies and outperforming existing state-of-the-art summarization techniques.

The article explores the neural architecture underlying language comprehension, highlighting the involvement of an extensive network of cortical regions beyond the traditionally emphasized left posterior temporal cortex. Through lesion and connectivity analyses, it identifies key brain areas and their interconnections, suggesting a complex network essential for understanding language processing in the brain.

The article discusses advancements in hierarchical neural story generation, detailing a system that utilizes a large dataset of 300,000 human-written stories to create coherent narratives from writing prompts. It highlights the model's innovative approach, which includes a novel fusion method and a gated multi-scale self-attention mechanism, resulting in superior performance in both automated and human evaluations compared to traditional non-hierarchical methods.

The article presents a novel unsupervised probabilistic framework called the joint sentiment/topic model (JST) for sentiment analysis, which simultaneously identifies sentiment and topics in text without the need for labeled data. Evaluated on a movie review dataset, preliminary experiments demonstrate that JST achieves promising results in classifying sentiment polarity.

The Handbook of Natural Language Processing, Second Edition offers updated practical tools and techniques for implementing natural language processing in computer systems, incorporating emerging topics like sentiment analysis while removing outdated material. This comprehensive resource serves as a valuable guide for professionals in computer science and linguistics, covering key areas such as text mining, language modeling, and machine learning.

The article discusses a novel technique for estimating relevance models in information retrieval using only the query, without the need for training data. It highlights the effectiveness of this approach in addressing challenges like synonymy and polysemy, demonstrating superior performance compared to baseline systems in TREC TDT tracking tasks.

The article presents a novel approach to language modeling using gated convolutional networks, which offers a more efficient alternative to traditional recurrent neural networks by enabling parallelization over sequential tokens. The proposed model achieves state-of-the-art performance on the WikiText-103 benchmark and competitive results on the Google Billion Words benchmark, significantly reducing latency while effectively capturing long-term dependencies.

The article provides an overview of the KL‐ONE knowledge representation system, highlighting its development, core concepts, and structured description capabilities. It discusses the system's philosophical foundations, taxonomy classification, and includes an example of its application in a classifier recognition task within the AI community.

The article presents a thorough analysis of noun categorization devices across various languages, exploring their grammatical mechanisms and implications for typology, morphosyntactic variation, and human cognition, making it relevant for linguists, anthropologists, and cognitive scientists.

edge.data: In 2018, article BERT: Pre-training of Deep Bidirectional Transformers for Language Understanding was published and referenced article Glove: Global Vectors for Word Representation that was published in 2014

The child paper, BERT, builds on the foundational concepts of word representation introduced in the parent paper, GloVe, by developing a more advanced language representation model that leverages deep bidirectional transformers for improved performance in various natural language processing tasks. While GloVe focuses on static word embeddings, BERT extends this work by enabling contextualized representations that can be fine-tuned for specific applications, thus inheriting and advancing the principles of word representation.
In 2018, article Deep Contextualized Word Representations was published and referenced article Glove: Global Vectors for Word Representation that was published in 2014

The child paper, "Deep Contextualized Word Representations," builds on the foundational concepts introduced in the parent paper, "GloVe: Global Vectors for Word Representation," by advancing the methodology of word representations in natural language processing. It extends the work of the parent paper by introducing deeper contextualized embeddings that enhance semantic understanding beyond the static representations provided by GloVe.
In 2019, article Sentence-BERT: Sentence Embeddings using Siamese BERT-Networks was published and referenced article Glove: Global Vectors for Word Representation that was published in 2014

The child paper, "Sentence-BERT: Sentence Embeddings using Siamese BERT-Networks," builds on the foundational concepts of word embeddings introduced in the parent paper, "GloVe: Global Vectors for Word Representation," by applying and extending these principles to create sentence embeddings using a Siamese architecture. While the parent paper focuses on word-level representations, the child paper advances the field by leveraging these embeddings for more complex sentence-level tasks in natural language processing.

In 2019, article BioBERT: a pre-trained biomedical language representation model for biomedical text mining was published and referenced article Glove: Global Vectors for Word Representation that was published in 2014

The child paper, "BioBERT," builds on the foundational concepts of word representation introduced in the parent paper, "GloVe," by adapting the BERT model for biomedical text mining tasks. It extends the application of word embeddings to a specific domain, demonstrating significant performance improvements in tasks such as named entity recognition and relation extraction, thereby inheriting and enhancing the methodologies established in the parent paper.

In 2019, article ALBERT: A Lite BERT for Self-supervised Learning of Language Representations was published and referenced article Glove: Global Vectors for Word Representation that was published in 2014

The child paper, "ALBERT: A Lite BERT for Self-supervised Learning of Language Representations," builds on the foundational concepts of word representations introduced in the parent paper, "Glove: Global Vectors for Word Representation," by applying advanced techniques to improve model efficiency and performance in natural language processing tasks. While the parent paper focuses on word vector representations, the child paper extends this work by introducing parameter-reduction methods and self-supervised learning strategies to enhance the BERT model's capabilities.
In 2020, article Unsupervised Cross-lingual Representation Learning at Scale was published and referenced article Glove: Global Vectors for Word Representation that was published in 2014

The child paper, "Unsupervised Cross-lingual Representation Learning at Scale," builds on the foundational concepts of word embeddings introduced in the parent paper, "Glove: Global Vectors for Word Representation," by applying and extending these vector space representations to cross-lingual contexts in natural language processing. It leverages the principles of word representation to enhance multilingual pretraining and cross-lingual retrieval tasks.
In 2015, article A large annotated corpus for learning natural language inference was published and referenced article Glove: Global Vectors for Word Representation that was published in 2014

The child paper builds on the parent paper by utilizing the word representation techniques developed in the GloVe model to enhance the performance of natural language inference tasks, specifically through the creation of a large annotated corpus. It extends the foundational work of the parent paper by applying its principles to a new domain, thereby demonstrating the practical utility of the word vectors in a novel context.

In 2018, article Recent Trends in Deep Learning Based Natural Language Processing [Review Article] was published and referenced article Glove: Global Vectors for Word Representation that was published in 2014
